name: Dokku Self-Hosted PaaS API
what_it_does: Open-source self-hosted PaaS with REST API — enabling developers to run a personal Heroku on any Linux server using Docker, with git-push deployments, buildpack support, automatic SSL via Let's Encrypt, environment variable management, managed databases via plugins (PostgreSQL, MySQL, Redis, MongoDB), and process management without managing Kubernetes.
use_cases: Deploying applications to own server via Dokku API for self-hosted git-push deployment workflow
Managing Dokku app environment variables via API for configuration automation
Provisioning Dokku plugin databases (PostgreSQL, Redis) via API for self-hosted database setup
Scaling Dokku app processes and worker counts via API for automated resource management
Managing Dokku domains and SSL certificates via API for custom domain automation
not_for: Teams without a server to run Dokku on (Dokku requires your own VPS or bare metal)
Enterprise workloads requiring high availability, load balancing across multiple nodes
Teams wanting managed infrastructure without server administration
best_when: A developer or small team wants a personal Heroku on their own VPS (DigitalOcean, Hetzner, Linode, etc.) — with git push deploys, zero monthly PaaS fees, and full data control.
avoid_when: You don't have server administration skills, need managed infrastructure, or require multi-node high availability.

auth.notes: SSH key authentication — not a traditional REST API. Dokku API is primarily SSH-based commands (dokku <command> executed over SSH). HTTP REST API available via dokku-api plugin (unofficial). No OAuth2. SSH key management via dokku ssh-keys commands. No webhooks natively — post-deploy hooks via Procfile or dokku-webhook-plugin. Self-hosted means you control all authentication.

pricing.free_tier_limits: Completely free open source (MIT license); only cost is your server
pricing.notes: Open source project. Created by Jeff Lindsay in 2013, inspired by Heroku. MIT license. 28,000+ GitHub stars. Written in bash and Go. Originally inspired by a mini-Heroku blog post by Heroku cofounder Adam Wiggins. Plugin ecosystem (100+ plugins) for databases, caching, etc. PostgreSQL, MySQL, Redis, MongoDB via plugins. Docker-based. Heroku buildpack support. Works on any Linux server. Single-server deployment — not clustered. Community-maintained plugins. Used by thousands of developers for self-hosted deployments.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: SSH-based command API — not traditional REST. Large open source community with good documentation. No rate limits (self-hosted). No webhooks natively. Requires server access for agent operations. HTTP REST API only via unofficial plugin. User controls all data and security. Good for agents with SSH access to the server.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: App reads idempotent by name. Config reads idempotent by key. Deployment via git push — idempotent for same commit. Plugin provision is idempotent.
known_agent_gotchas: SSH key authentication — not REST API — agents need SSH access to the Dokku server
No built-in REST API — use dokku-api plugin for HTTP access (unofficial, limited)
Single-server deployment — no high availability or load balancing
No webhooks natively — deploy hooks require server-side scripts
Plugin ecosystem quality varies — some plugins are unmaintained

security_score_components.security_notes: SSH key authentication (strong auth mechanism). User-controlled data residency and compliance. Let's Encrypt automatic SSL. Environment variables stored on server (user-controlled encryption). Open source — auditable code. SSH access controls provide server-level security. User responsible for server hardening. No centralized secret management — secrets in Dokku config. Plugin security depends on plugin quality.
